one_line_takeaway: OpenRAG-Soc is a benchmark (not a new attack or defense) showing that combining HTML/Markdown sanitization, Unicode normalization, and attribution-gated prompting drives Social-Web indirect-prompt-injection ASR from a 24.9% vanilla macro-average down to 4.7%, with defense ordering holding even under adaptive real-web attacks, at low latency/utility cost.
key_finding: Vanilla (no defense) yields the highest per-carrier ASR (macro-avg 24.9%, 95% CI 20.3-29.2), with Hidden spans highest at 34.0% (27.0-41.0) and ARIA lowest at 9.6% (7.2-12.4). Sanitization mainly suppresses HTML/Markdown carriers (macro-avg ASR drops to 13.1%) but barely touches zero-width attacks (23.0% vs 23.2% vanilla); Normalization mainly suppresses zero-width (7.8% vs 23.2% vanilla) but barely touches HTML carriers. Combining all three defenses (Sanitized+Normalized+Attribution-gated) is consistently lowest, macro-avg ASR 4.7% (3.5-6.3), across both sparse and dense retrievers, at negligible utility cost (answerability changed by -1.8pp/-2.2pp) and modest latency overhead (sanitization +3.1% median / +154ms p95; normalization <0.5%). On the clean set at k=10, Sanitized+Normalized nearly preserved ranking quality (MRR@10: 0.462->0.450, delta -0.012; nDCG@10: 0.598->0.589, delta -0.009). Residual-failure bucketing attributes 49% of remaining ASR to visible imperatives (best mitigated by attribution, -78% relative), 31% to confusables in code/pre blocks (best mitigated by normalization, -66%), and 20% to query-alignment cases where the user query mirrors the injected task (best mitigated by sanitization+attribution, -61%). On a real-web crawl (N=2,350 pages), adaptive/red-team payloads raise ASR over static payloads by +1.1 to +6.2 percentage points across all defense settings but preserve the same defense ordering (Vanilla > Normalized > Sanitized > All Defenses), and All-Defenses ASR remains low (4.3% static / 5.4% adaptive).
core_contribution: The paper introduces OpenRAG-Soc, a compact, reproducible benchmark-and-harness (4-page WWW'26 paper) for evaluating Web-facing RAG systems against Social-Web indirect prompt injection (IPI) and retrieval poisoning. It packages: (1) a 6,200-page Web-native corpus spanning five injection carriers (hidden spans, off-screen CSS, alt text, ARIA, zero-width Unicode), each with a payload/control counterpart, plus a small (~200-page) PDF text-layer/SVG title/desc slice; (2) an ingest->retrieve->generate harness with interchangeable sparse (BM25/Pyserini) and dense (E5-base-v2, BGE-large-en-v1.5, Contriever-like) retrievers and multiple open-weight/API generator LLMs (Llama-3-8B/70B, Mistral-7B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B); (3) three deployable, composable defenses -- HTML/Markdown sanitization (DOMPurify-based), Unicode normalization (NFKC + control stripping), and attribution-gated (quote-and-cite) prompting; and (4) a paired metric suite reporting attack success rate (ASR) at answer time alongside retrieval rank-shift (delta MRR@10, delta nDCG@10) under poisoning, plus utility and latency costs, aggregated over >=8k runs per configuration.
threat_model: Attacker controls a subset of Web pages that get ingested by the retriever and knows the available markup carriers (hidden spans, off-screen CSS, alt text, ARIA, zero-width/confusable Unicode) but has no access to model weights or the system prompt. The victim system performs top-k retrieval (default k=5) feeding a single LLM. Attacker success is defined two ways: (i) instruction execution at answer time (ASR), or (ii) elevated retrieval rank for attacker-targeted documents under poisoning (measured via delta MRR@10 / delta nDCG@10).
limitations: The authors explicitly scope out: (1) fully prompt-aware/adaptive adversaries who know the exact defensive prompt (their adaptive-payload test uses a fixed red-team prompt set, not adversary knowledge of the system prompt); (2) multi-stage pipelines with JS-rendered DOM transforms, custom renderers, or OCR noise, which may introduce carriers/failure modes not evaluated here; (3) the detector-based ASR measurement carries residual bias (bounded at +-0.8pp by human-only re-scoring) and misses paraphrased/multi-sentence obedience (65% of false negatives) and cross-sentence dependencies (35%); (4) the paper itself notes 'absolute numbers may certainly vary as a result of chunking/retrievers' -- only the relative defense ordering is claimed as robust; (5) it is a 4-page WWW'26 short/benchmark paper, so defenses evaluated (sanitization, normalization, attribution-gating, and one SelfRAG-style critique/regenerate baseline) are existing, deployable techniques repackaged and measured together rather than a new defense mechanism.

datasets_used: OpenRAG-Soc synthetic corpus: 6,200 social-style pages across 5 carriers (hidden spans 1,330; off-screen CSS 1,280; alt text 1,280; ARIA 980; zero-width 1,330), 3,090 visible / 3,110 hidden-or-off-screen variants, plus ~200-page PDF text-layer/SVG title-desc slice
Real-web stress-test crawl: N=2,350 permissibly crawled blogs/docs/forums pages via breadth-2 crawling, per-domain cap 105 pages
Human annotation sets for detector validation: N=1,200 triple-labeled generations; N=300 focused paraphrase/cross-sentence audit; N=750 audit for detector-uncertainty bootstrap
quantitative_results: Macro-avg ASR: Vanilla 24.9% (20.3-29.2) -> Sanitized 13.1% (10.0-16.2) -> Normalized 21.3% (17.0-25.2) -> All Defenses 4.7% (3.5-6.3)
Per-carrier Vanilla ASR: Hidden spans 34.0%, Off-screen CSS 30.1%, Alt text 27.8%, ARIA 9.6%, Zero-width 23.2%
Clean-set k=10 ranking: MRR@10 0.462->0.450 (delta -0.012), nDCG@10 0.598->0.589 (delta -0.009) under Sanitized+Normalized vs Vanilla
Utility cost of Sanitized+Normalized: answerability -1.8pp/-2.2pp; attribution-gated token-level citation alignment 0.88
ASR detector validation: P=0.92, R=0.89, F1=0.90, Cohen's kappa=0.84 (N=1,200); confusion matrix TP=498/FP=43/FN=62/TN=797
Latency: Vanilla end-to-end 1,005ms/2,080ms (median/p95); Sanitized +31/+154ms; Normalized +5/+10ms; sanitization adds 3.1% median (p95 7.4%) to pipeline latency, normalization <0.5%
Real-web static vs adaptive ASR: Vanilla 22.7%->28.9% (+6.2pp), Normalized 19.1%->22.2% (+3.1pp), Sanitized 12.0%->15.8% (+3.8pp), All Defenses 4.3%->5.4% (+1.1pp)

Failure-mode buckets of residual ASR: visible imperatives 49% (attribution reduces 78%), confusables in code 31% (normalization reduces 66%), query alignment 20% (sanitization+attribution reduces 61%)
Chunk size {256,512,768} changes absolute ASR by at most 2.2pp (median) without altering configuration ordering; top-k 3->10 mildly increases HTML-carrier ASR but keeps All Defenses within 4%+-1%
SelfRAG-style critique/regenerate baseline cuts ASR by 0.6pp over All Defenses at +70ms median overhead and -1.1pp answerability
Stronger dense models improve clean ranking (MRR@10 +0.018 vs E5-base) without changing defense ordering
baselines_compared: Vanilla (no sanitization, no normalization, standard prompting)
Sanitized (HTML/Markdown sanitization via DOMPurify-style production sanitizer)
Attribution-gated (quote-and-cite prompting constraining outputs to cited spans)
SelfRAG-style critique/regenerate baseline compared against All Defenses
BM25 sparse retriever vs. dense retrievers (E5-base-v2, BGE-large-en-v1.5, Contriever-like)
Generator models: Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B, Llama-3-70B
